steps.title: Identify what the claim requires
steps.content: The underlined claim has three parts: the rider must be (1) experienced, (2) mainly biking to work, and (3) preferring routes that reduce travel time. A supporting response must satisfy all three parts simultaneously.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice against the claim
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘“I have a bike, but I don’t ride it very often. When the weather is nice, I sometimes use my bike to go into town to do some shopping.”’: The rider is infrequent and bikes for errands, not work — fails parts 1 and 2. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘“I just got a new bike, and I’m looking forward to going on rides with my friends soon.”’: Doesn’t establish experience, doesn’t mention commuting to work, and states no route preference. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘“I bike to my job every day. There’s a nice bike path that goes through a park, but I use the bike lane on the main road because it’s faster.”’: Daily commuting implies experience (part 1), bikes to work (part 2), and explicitly chooses the faster route over the scenic one (part 3). ✓
  • Choice D — ‘“I usually bike to work, but I’m taking the bus now because my bike has a broken part.”’: The rider is an experienced work commuter but gives no route preference — fails part 3. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
